pop_up_instruction: No social group is mentioned in the target sentence.

A social group is mentioned, but the mention is descriptive. The group is not invoked to justify, criticize, support, oppose, or motivate a policy position. E.g. ``Immigrants live in Germany.'' A group appeal occurs when a social group is used as part of the argument, not merely mentioned. The group may be presented as affected by a problem, causing a problem, deserving praise or blame, or as the target of a proposed action.Examples: ``Immigrants are struggling’’ (problem), ``Because immigrants do X...’’ (cause), ``Working mothers deserve support’’ (evaluation), ``We should help Roma families’’ (remedy).
If two or more groups were selected, also tick the intersectional flag.
Flag sentences containing appeals to several separate groups

Use this flag when groups are explicitly contrasted, compared, or set against each other. Common markers: ``vs.'', ``compared to'', ``wherea'', ``unlike'', ``rather than'', ``as opposed to'' parallel constructions: ``immigrant men … native women …''

Use when a group is referred to with a derogatory, insulting, stigmatizing, or slang term (e.g. ``NAFRI'' instead of ``Nordafrikaner'' or  ``Asylant'' instead of ``Asylsuchender'', ``Welfare queen'' instead of ``welfare recipient''). The social group should be coded as usual; this flag captures only the use of the pejorative label.Note that regional labels can change connotation depending on the context. For example: Geordie (Newcastle). Brummie (Birmingham), Scouser (Liverpool), Yankees (Amerikans), Paddy and Mick for Irish, Jock for Scottish, Taff for Welsh, Ossi for East Germans, etc

ethnic / ethno-cultural minority; does not require a MIG_* code. If religion is used as an ethnic proxy, also assign religion category. Do not infer ethnicity from names, passports, languages, or locations alone. Use this group only when the text itself constructs the group as an ethnic or ethno-cultural category.
explicitly named ethnic or national origin group; does not require a MIG_* code

first-generation migrants, foreigners (unless context is purely nationality. If also framed ethnically, add “ethnic minority”. Assign migration groups only when migration is part of the group definition. Separate migration identity from legal status. Do not force a migration code on ethnic minorities without migration background.
asylum seekers or refugees
second-generation migrants, descendants of immigrants
co-ethnic return migrants, repatriates, ancestry-based migrant identities

dominant ethno-national group, majority / hegemonic group, national identities, citizens of the country; Majority is especially relevant in oppositional constructions (ON) where the in-group is contrasted with immigrants or ethnic minorities.

other religious affiliations. If a religious labels functions as ethnic or cultural group markers rather than references to faith, assign both the religion and ethnicity gropus.
people living in cities. Use only when the group is defined by place, not incidental location
people living in rural areas. Use only when the group is defined by place, not incidental location

The argument about the group is based primarily on material resources, jobs, wages, skills, productivity, economic performance, taxes, public spending, welfare costs, fiscal sustainability, or economic incentives. Examples: labour shortages, unemployment, wage competition, tax burden, benefit dependency, productivity, growth, public budgets.

The argument about the group is based primarily on identity, values, norms, religion, language, traditions, belonging, social cohesion, national identity, cultural compatibility, or symbolic boundaries. Examples: protecting national culture, shared values, integration, family values, social cohesion, who belongs, threats to identity.

The argument relies on both economic and cultural reasoning, and neither dimension can be removed without substantially changing the justification. Example: Immigration depresses wages and undermines our way of life., We need skilled migrants to support the economy, but they must adopt our values.
The text contains a group appeal, but the reasoning cannot be reliably classified as economic or cultural, or there is insufficient information to determine the primary basis of the argument.

The group is presented positively or as deserving support, protection, inclusion, resources, rights, recognition, or improved outcomes. Examples: Immigrants make an important contribution to society., We should invest more in disadvantaged children.

The group is presented negatively or as deserving criticism, blame, exclusion, restrictions, reduced support, or worse outcomes. Examples: We must restrict access for asylum seekers., Benefits for the unemployed should be reduced.

The group is framed both positively and negatively, or both supported and restricted, such that no clear overall polarity can be assigned. Use only when neither pro-group nor anti-group clearly dominates. Example: We must support unemployed people, but benefits should be made more conditional.

The speaker accepts and relies on the group appeal. The group-based reasoning is presented as valid and used to support the argument. Example: Working families are struggling, so they should get tax relief.

The speaker disputes, rejects, or invalidates the group appeal. The group-based reasoning is presented as incorrect, unfair, or irrelevant. Example: It’s not immigrants causing housing shortages—it’s zoning.

The speaker partially accepts the group appeal but limits, modifies, reframes, or contests it. Examples: Immigration may affect wages in some sectors, but it is not the main driver., I agree low-income families need support, but this policy targets the wrong ones.
The speaker's stance toward the group appeal cannot be determined.
